title: Buying and storing livestock concentrates
subtitle: Getting the right feed, buying at the right time, and keeping vermin out of the store.
excerpt: Concentrates for sheep, goats, pigs, and poultry are the most expensive line in the feed budget. Buying in bulk at the right time of year saves a meaningful amount; storing them incorrectly loses that saving to moisture, mould, and vermin. Here's how to buy smart and keep what you buy.
sourceNotes: AHDB Beef and Lamb, Feed Management. DEFRA/APHA animal feed storage and contamination guidance. SAC Consulting Scotland, Concentrate Feeding on Small Farms.

glossaryTerms.definition: A supplementary feed containing a higher proportion of energy and protein than hay or pasture alone; includes compound nuts, rolls, and straight grains such as rolled barley or sugar beet pulp.
Feeding where animals have constant unrestricted access to feed; suitable for hay and some supplements but not for most concentrates, which cause overconsumption and digestive problems.

content.text: feed that's been in a damp shed all summer tastes of mould, may contain mycotoxins, and will be refused by well-fed stock. Buying the right product, in the right quantity, and storing it correctly from the delivery date is what separates an efficient feed budget from an expensive one.

Compound feeds are balanced for a species and stage of production. A ewe nut formulated for late pregnancy has different energy and calcium levels than a maintenance-stage sheep nut. Using a high-energy product on stock not in late pregnancy or lactation wastes money and can cause excess body condition. Match the label to your current production stage.

Buy enough to last six to eight weeks at most; compounds lose palatability and some vitamin potency after about eight weeks of storage once the bag is opened. In a bulk sack, opened feed deteriorates faster than in unopened 25 kg bags. If you feed concentrates year-round, a rolling monthly order works better than a large annual purchase.
Store in a dry, ventilated building on pallets, pallets keep sacks off the damp floor and allow air circulation underneath.
If using a feed bin, empty and clean it before each delivery. Pouring fresh feed on top of old residue carries over any contamination.
Keep sacks sealed with a clip or tie between uses. Moisture from humid air makes compound feeds cake and promotes mould growth.

Rats and mice spread Salmonella through feed; contaminated concentrate is a disease risk to both animals and the people who handle the feed. Site the feed store in a building with no ground-level gaps. Plastic or galvanised steel bins are better than wooden ones, rats chew through wood but not metal.
concentrates to sheep, goats, or pigs in a way that leaves feed in an open trough overnight. That draws rodents and risks contamination.

Record the delivery date, batch number, and type of feed for each delivery. If you have an animal health problem, the feed batch number lets your vet rule out feed contamination as a cause. Most feed bags include a best-before date, follow it.
